## Success Criteria

- [ ] WHEN THE GATE IS ENABLED (the config preference in SC#4 below; DEFAULT OFF), claiming a ticket
      (open→in_progress) requires a valid, non-stale signed plan-review attestation; absence or a
      material-edit-invalidated signature blocks the claim, with a recorded `--force` override available.
      (Default OFF ⇒ `claim` keeps today's behavior; the requirement is opt-in — see SC#4.)
- [ ] The deterministic Layer-1 floor blocks only on sound, unambiguous checks and fails open on any unsupported
      language/tool (skipped = pass), with coverage recorded on the attestation.
- [ ] Layer-2 review returns coaching findings that are structurally well-formed — each carries criterion id,
      file/section location, severity, a checklist line, and a suggested fix when confident — verifiable in-session
      against the REVIEW_RESULT schema; judgment criteria are advisory by default. (Remediation-rate uplift is
      tracked OFFLINE as follow-on F1, not an in-session gate.)
- [ ] A project can configure risk posture, chunking, budget caps, and per-criterion blocking via config, with
      sensible defaults shipped, INCLUDING a config PREFERENCE that turns the plan-review requirement ON/OFF
      for claiming a ticket or transitioning it to in_progress. The preference DEFAULTS OFF when unspecified;
      'rollback' is simply turning the preference off — an ordinary config preference, not a deploy-time
      kill-switch, and no separate rollback mechanism is needed.
- [ ] Every review emits a `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar event sufficient to reconstruct per-criterion FP/remediation
      analysis offline, without taxing rebar's hot paths.
- [ ] The signed attestation is checkable by an external CI process as a rigor/anti-vibe-coding signal.

The 50ms fast-claim target's in-session PROVING CHECK is STRUCTURAL — child `092b`'s 'No LLM/network on the claim path' AC (a test asserts the claim path makes NO LLM and NO network call, a pure local HMAC verify) — NOT a timed run; the actual p50/p95 is then captured via the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar metadata for passive refinement.

## Experiment-grounded defaults (round 3; config-overridable)

Grounded by 5 experiment rounds (~1,600 LLM runs). Full design: docs/experiments/plan-review-criteria-storage-and-chunking.md.

- **Execution tiers are the primary axis (not chunk size).** DET (P1–P8) = parallel code, ~free. SINGLE-TURN
  (F1,F4,E2,E5,G5,T5a–e) = chunkable LLM calls, ~$0.005–0.008/criterion cached. 2-STEP (E1,E3,E6) = two ordered
  calls. AGENT/tool-using (E4,G1G2,A1,T1,T6–T8) = one agent loop per criterion, ~$0.12 + ~50s each (≈85× a
  single-turn call) — NOT chunkable; gated by proportionate scrutiny.
- **Criteria storage:** each descriptor declares `exec` (tier), `facet` (the concern/context it examines — the
  advisory grouping key), `context_needs`, `routing`, `trigger`. The chunker is generic: filter → bucket by tier
  → pack same-`facet` single-turn criteria up to the chunk size → one agent per AGENT criterion → DET as code.
  Groupings are advisory (facet tags re-pack at any chunk size); adding/tuning a criterion only declares exec+facet.
- **Chunk size = base_chunk(model) × size_factor(ticket), within the single-turn tier.** base_chunk = {opus 12,
  sonnet 6 with coherent facet packing (3 without), haiku/local 3}; size_factor = {trivial/moderate 1.0,
  large|epic|has_children 0.5}; clamp [2, n]. → Opus packs ~all single-turn criteria in one cached call (its
  consistency is flat in N); Sonnet 6 (coherent) / 3 (large); never the full set on the largest plans.
- **Enable prompt caching** (system+plan as the cached prefix): collapses the cost gap between chunk sizes (1.55×
  vs 2.6×), so chunk for reliability not cost. Cached fine-chunk Sonnet (~$0.07–0.09/review) ≈ one Opus pass
  (~$0.10) — prefer Opus for max consistency/big-plan stability, Sonnet+cache+coherent for the cost floor.
- **Budget (generous, per-plan):** cap = $0.15 (DET+single-turn base) + n_agentic_fired × $0.25 + (container criteria G3/G4 fan out ONE agent call per CHILD when they fire: + n_children × $0.25) + headroom (small
  task ~$0.15–0.40; heavy epic ~$1.5–2.0 + minutes). INDETERMINATE on cap-hit sheds lowest-priority AGENT/overlay
  criteria first (85× cost), never the cheap base. The cap is PER REVIEW RUN; prompt caching saves only WITHIN a run (the stable system+rubric+plan prefix across criteria), and the edit-then-re-review CONVERGENCE LOOP (~2–3 runs) BUSTS that prefix each round — so the budgeted cost-to-signature ≈ cap × revisions (NO cross-run cache saving assumed); the per-run cost is captured on the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar so the real convergence cost is observed, not benchmarked.
- **Criteria are fully specified from DSO source** (docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/criteria/dso-criteria-specs.md), not one-liners,
  and the set is >12 (single-turn ~13 + agentic + overlays + DET P1–P8).

## Design decisions revisited (round 5; SETTLED in the brainstorm)

A round-5 external review re-raised two design questions — but both were already the brainstorm's central debates (session log 63cc, DISCUSSION NARRATIVE + final-decisions entry), resolved with explicit reasoning; they are NOT open.
(1) **Blocking on `claim` vs coaching** — the brainstorm explicitly weighed A blocking-inline / B advisory / C decoupled-signed-attestation and chose **C**: `claim` is a FAST succeed/fail check for a signed 'ready' attestation, the heavy review runs out-of-band, `--force`+justification is the escape. The 'hard-gate-is-anti-pattern' research was examined and found NOT to transfer to autonomous agents (which confabulate rather than ask, and where early errors compound; separate-critic >> self-critique). The minimal sound DET floor is the only hard blocker; Layer-2 never blocks. (2) **What the signature certifies** — an Opus adversarial review in the brainstorm raised the same 'fail-open hides a hollow signal' critique; the resolution is that the cert means 'process followed, no red flags' (NOT 'perfect') WITH **coverage recorded**, the rich per-criterion verdicts/fingerprints live in the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar, and a project composes any hard CI gate by checking the signed result + coverage. The brainstorm also explicitly rejected block-by-default-with-routine-override (>30% FP → tool ignored) in favor of advisory-Layer-2.
The genuinely-useful RESIDUAL from the round-5 review (tuning, not redesign): state end-to-end time-to-first-work honestly (not just the 50ms claim check); the prompt-cache busts on the edit-then-re-review loop (re-derive that saving); and make the CI rigor predicate canonical in-repo rather than leaving each client to define it. Notably the review's findings map to COH (a criterion we'd dropped) and G6 (the one we added) — evidence the registry is on track.

## Scope clarification — calibration & tier-tuning are POST-IMPLEMENTATION (not pre-impl blockers)

- THRESHOLD CALIBRATION / eval-driven tuning is explicitly FOLLOW-ON, OUT OF SCOPE for v1 (mirrors `9da1`):
  calibration is only meaningful against the RUNNING system, so v1 ships intentionally HIGH thresholds
  (advisory-by-default, near-zero blocking) and the standing eval suite + the `REVIEW_RESULT` sidecar collect
  REAL data during dogfooding to tune them later. The eval-suite labeled cases ARE in scope (sanity-check the
  criteria); deriving/tuning block thresholds FROM them is post-implementation. It is NOT an implementation
  prerequisite.
- TIER RE-VALIDATION for the borderline criteria (T11 migration grounding; T5a/T5b perf/reliability single-
  turn-vs-agent) is POST-EPIC fine-tuning, not a pre-impl spike — revisit with eval/dogfood data.

## Design clarifications (review-surfaced: T9 claim atomicity, T8 DET execution path)
- CLAIM ATOMICITY (T9): the fast signature-verify and the open→in_progress transition are ONE atomic step,
  reusing rebar's EXISTING atomic `claim` primitive (re-read under lock + optimistic concurrency /
  ConcurrencyError) — NOT a check-then-act sequence. So two agents cannot both pass the signature check and
  both claim; cross-clone double-claims converge per rebar's existing replay semantics.
- DET EXECUTION PATH (T8): the deterministic floor (P1–P8) is a FIRST-CLASS exec tier (`exec=DET`) the
  orchestrator dispatches via a CODE executor alongside the 1-TURN / 2-STEP / AGENT (LLM) tiers — so the DET
  checks are actually RUN, not merely named in the registry.

## Design clarification — AGENT-tier tool-access contract + dep-graph-need ⇒ agent-tier (e6d9 dry-run-surfaced)

A dry run of this gate on the workflow-engine-v2 epic (`e6d9`) produced false positives whose common root was
that the reviewer was fed ticket TEXT + repo grep but NOT the broader ticket DEPENDENCY GRAPH: it flagged a
"missing sequencing edge" that already existed (a `depends_on` link between two children) and an event-schema
"migration risk" a v1 fact ruled out. Two design consequences:

- ROUTING RULE — dep-graph-need is an agent-tier SIGNAL: a criterion that needs the broader ticket dependency
  graph (or any cross-ticket / cross-artifact context) MUST be AGENT-tier (tool-using), NOT single-turn
  fed-context. An injected snapshot is necessarily incomplete and yields partial-view FPs. Container/cross-ticket
  criteria are reclassified accordingly: G3 (child coverage) and G4 (child consistency) move 1-TURN -> AGENT;
  COH's cross-ticket coherence likewise requires the live graph when run at container altitude. (T12
  rollout/rollback stays 1-TURN LLM-routed per `criteria_v8` — its `deploy_ordering` sub-check reads the plan's
  STATED producer/consumer changes, not the live graph, so it is NOT in the agent-tier set.) (This reverses the earlier "G3/G4 are ticket-analysis, fed as context"
  correction: the dry run showed fed context is incomplete — the criterion must READ the graph live.)
- AGENT TOOL-ACCESS CONTRACT (always): every AGENT-tier criterion's tool access ALWAYS includes (1) rebar — the
  ticket store: show/list/deps/ready/next_batch/links/children, i.e. the LIVE dependency graph and hierarchy,
  not a snapshot; and (2) the FILE SYSTEM (grep/read/glob). Crucially, rebar + filesystem access also GRANTS
  access to any REFERENCED ARTIFACTS OR ANALYSIS the ticket cites: linked session logs and related/blocking
  tickets (via rebar show/deps), and referenced docs/experiments reports, research, or design docs (via
  read_file/glob). The agent follows the references; it is never limited to the ticket's own prose.
